sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 146, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 145 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 5.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the layout more clearly:
- Rows 26-28: RRR (red) followed by WWWWWWWWW (white) - this is the top part
- Rows 32-34: NNN (green) followed by RRR (red) followed by WWWWWWWWW (white) - this is the middle part
- Rows 35-37: RRR (red) followed by NNN (green) followed by SSS (sky blue) followed by NNN (green) followed by SSS (sky blue) followed by WWWWWWWWW (white) - this is the lower middle part
- Rows 41-43: RRR (red) followed by NNN (green) followed by SSS (sky blue) followed by NNN (green) - this is the bottom part
Looking at this, I see:
- There are 4 groups of red blocks (RRR), each with 9 pixels (3x3).
- There are green blocks (NNN) forming a structure.
- There are white blocks (WWWWWWWWW) forming a structure to the right.
The character seems to be composed of:
- Red blocks (RRR) - the feet
- Green blocks (NNN) - the body
The white blocks (WWWWWWWWW) form a separate structure to the right.
Looking at the current state:
- The character is upright with red feet at the top (rows 26-28).
- The green body is below the red feet (rows 32-34, 35-37, 41-43).
- The white structure is to the right of the character.
Wait, I think I'm misunderstanding the layout. Let me look more carefully.
Rows 26-28: RRR followed by WWWWWWWWW
Rows 32-34: NNN followed by RRR followed by WWWWWWWWW
Rows 35-37: RRR followed by NNN followed by SSS followed by NNN followed by SSS followed by WWWWWWWWW
Rows 41-43: RRR followed by NNN followed by SSS followed by NNN
This is a complex layout. Let me think about what the character and green frame represent.
Actually, I think the character is the green structure (NNN) with red feet (RRR). The white structure (WWWWWWWWW) is the target area or green frame.
Looking at the current image, the character is upright with red feet at the top. The green frame (white structure) is to the right of the character. The character's body is inside the green frame. The red feet are to the left of the green frame.
Wait, but the white structure is to the right, not forming a frame around the character. Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking at the ASCII, the white blocks (WWWWWWWWW) are to the right of the character in all rows. This suggests the white structure is a vertical strip to the right of the character.
The green blocks (NNN) form a structure that looks like a frame or a container. The red blocks (RRR) are attached to the green structure.
I think the character is the green structure with red feet, and the white structure is a separate object (maybe a target or a wall).
Let me think about what the goal could be. Maybe the goal is to have the character's red feet align with the white structure. But the white structure is to the right, and the red feet are to the left or top.
Actually, I just realized something. Looking at the current image, the character is upright with red feet at the top. The green frame is to the right of the character. The character's body is inside the green frame. The red feet are to the left of the green frame.
Wait, I think the "green frame" I've been seeing is actually the white structure, not the green structure. Let me reconsider.
Looking at the current image:
- There's a green structure (which could be the character's body).
- There are red blocks (which could be the character's red feet).
- There's a white structure (which could be the green frame or target area).
I think the character is the green structure with red feet, and the white structure is the target area.
The goal might be to have the character move into the white structure. But the white structure is to the right, and the character is to the left.
Let me try moving the character RIGHT to see if it moves into the white structure.
But I tried moving RIGHT before, and it didn't work (no_impact: true). This suggests the character is blocked from moving RIGHT.
Let me try moving the character in a different direction. Maybe I need to move the character UP or DOWN first, then RIGHT.
Actually, I just realized something. I've been assuming that the character moves when I press UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT. But maybe the character doesn't move; maybe the camera or the view moves.
Let me check if the character's position changes when I press UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Actually, looking at the last action result, the last action was `no_impact: true` for the RIGHT moves. This means the board didn't change. So the character didn't move.
Let me try moving the character UP to see if that changes the board.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try moving the character UP and see if the board changes
result = action(['UP'])
print(f"UP result: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={result.get('no_impact')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
